text.title: jenkins-pipeline-patterns Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for jenkins-pipeline-patterns as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Writing entire pipeline logic inside a single large scripted block because "Groovy is more powerful than Declarative".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Scripted blocks hide logic from CI/CD governance, syntax checking, and team review. Build errors reference internal CPS class names (org.jenkinsci.plugins.workflow.cps.CpsThread) that agents misinterpret as application errors. Maintenance burden increases dramatically when the next team member touches the pipeline. The pipeline becomes unmaintainable as team size grows. New team members cannot understand the flow without running the build and reading error messages.

failure-mode.description: Storing shared library code directly in the application repository alongside Jenkinsfiles instead of a dedicated library repo.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Complicates versioning and testing. When the application repo is cloned for a build, the library code is checked out at the same commit, preventing independent rollback. Multi-team governance becomes impossible. Updates to the library must coordinate with application releases, slowing down fix deployment. A library bug in shared library breaks all 50+ pipelines simultaneously with no quick rollback path.

failure-mode.description: Parallel branches sharing the same workspace without `dir()` or `ws()` isolation, causing file races.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Jenkins shares workspace by default across parallel branches. A unit test writing to `target/` and integration test reading from `target/` simultaneously causes corruption. Always use `dir('unique-dir-${env.EXECUTOR_NUMBER}')` or separate workspaces.

failure-mode.description: Omitting `timeout` and `buildDiscarder`, relying on manual Jenkins administration to kill stuck builds and archive old logs.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Leaves the system vulnerable to queue starvation and disk exhaustion. New team members do not understand the cleanup rules.

failure-mode.description: Using static node labels (agent { label 'ubuntu-builder' }) on-prem instead of Kubernetes pod templates.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Static labels require manual node provisioning and do not scale. Pod templates auto-scale with cluster load and isolate resource contention per-pipeline.

failure-mode.description: Logging environment variables inside `withCredentials` or using `sh -x` to debug, which leaks secrets despite masking.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Even though Jenkins masks values in final output, intermediate debug logs and log aggregation systems may capture unmasked values. The pipeline becomes a vector for credential theft.

failure-mode.description: Omitting `post { always { cleanWs() } }` because "Jenkins will clean up eventually".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Workspace accumulates over hundreds of builds, filling disk very quickly. Operator intervention becomes urgent. The pipeline becomes slow to start due to workspace size.

failure-mode.description: Allowing agents to push Jenkinsfile changes directly to main and then run the build to "validate in real-time".
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Pollutes build history with generated/test builds. Slow feedback loop. If the generated Jenkinsfile has a credential leak, it is already committed.

failure-mode.description: Using Pattern or Matcher objects directly in the main pipeline flow without `@NonCPS` wrapping.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Compiles fine, but the CPS transformer cannot serialize the Pattern object. At a random checkpoint (after a parallel stage, after a timeout, after a plugin API call), the build fails with `NotSerializableException: java.util.regex.Pattern`. The error is opaque and difficult to diagnose.
